mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 2 Series 214d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 214d (B37C15A) engine typically costs £2,200–£4,500 supply only. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on workshop location. Main dealer new engines can exceed £5,500 supply only. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 2 Series 214d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B37 three-cylinder diesel is generally regarded as a reliable and efficient engine. It has relatively few major failure points compared to some other BMW engines. However, it is sensitive to regular maintenance, particularly oil changes. Common issues include EGR clogging (especially with urban driving), turbocharger wear, and fuel injector problems.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 2 Series 214d?

acceptedAnswer.text: EGR system clogging (rough idling, decreased efficiency), turbocharger wear (loss of power, smoke), fuel injector issues (hesitation, misfires), and oil leaks from various seals and gaskets. The EGR system is the most common issue, particularly on vehicles used for short journeys.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 2 Series 214d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% BMW scrap threshold: if your 2 Series is worth £8,000 and a recon engine is £3,500 (44%), it's worth it. If the vehicle is a tired £5,000 example, the same engine exceeds 60% — get a scrap valuation first. These are practical family vehicles that often justify replacement spend if the rest of the car is in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 2 Series 214d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 214d uses the B37C15A — a 1.5-litre three-cylinder diesel producing 95 PS. It was fitted to the Active Tourer (F45) and Gran Tourer (F46) from 2015 to 2018. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the EGR system, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The EGR valve can often be cleaned rather than replaced (£200–£600). Regular maintenance and using quality fuel can help prevent clogging. If the valve is heavily clogged or damaged, replacement may be necessary. Ignoring EGR issues can lead to reduced performance and increased emissions.

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 2 Series 214d engine typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The compact 3-cylinder layout makes the engine relatively straightforward to access compared to larger BMW units.

problems.title: EGR System Clogging
problems.symptoms: Rough idling, decreased fuel efficiency, increased emissions, engine warning light.
problems.typicalMileage: 30,000–60,000 miles — can clog earlier with frequent short journeys.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring it can lead to reduced performance and increased emissions.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The EGR system on the B37 engine can become clogged over time, particularly if the vehicle is used predominantly for short urban journeys. This can lead to rough idling, decreased fuel efficiency, and increased emissions. Regular maintenance and cleaning of the EGR system can help mitigate these issues. If you notice any of these symptoms, have the EGR system inspected promptly.

problems.title: Turbocharger Wear
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, increased exhaust smoke, unusual noises from the engine bay.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, turbo failure can lead to engine damage.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: Some B37 owners have reported premature turbocharger wear or failure. Symptoms include a sudden drop in performance, increased exhaust smoke, or unusual noises from the engine bay. The variable geometry turbocharger is a critical component, and failure can lead to significant engine damage if not addressed promptly. Regular oil changes with the correct specification are essential for turbo longevity.

problems.title: Fuel Injector Issues
problems.symptoms: Rough idling, misfires, decreased fuel efficiency, hesitation during acceleration.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: Fuel injectors can become clogged or fail, leading to poor engine performance and increased fuel consumption. If you experience hesitation during acceleration or notice a decrease in fuel efficiency, it may be worth having the injectors checked. Using quality fuel and regular servicing can help prevent injector issues.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 214d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
* EGR system recently cleaned or replaced
* No engine warning lights or DPF issues
avoidIf: * Engine warning light with no diagnosis
* Poor service history — B37 engines are sensitive to oil quality
* DPF warning or evidence of DPF issues
* Rough running or hesitation — potential injector or EGR issues

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The B37 is a generally reliable and efficient engine, but it requires regular maintenance to stay that way. EGR clogging is the most common issue, particularly on cars used for short journeys. A £3,500 reconditioned engine on a 2 Series valued at £8,000 is 44% of the car's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. On a sub-£5,000 example, think carefully. The 70% BMW scrap threshold applies.

cta.label: → Compare 2 Series 214d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 2 Series 214d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any unusual rattles or noises on startup
* Service History — Regular oil changes using the correct BMW specification
* DPF Status — Check for DPF warning lights; short journeys accelerate clogging
* EGR System — Any rough idling or engine warning lights? EGR may be clogged
* Turbo Check — Test acceleration; any whistling, smoke, or power loss?
* Smoke Check — Excessive smoke could indicate turbo or injector issues
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
